aliases: Spicy tofu minced pork dry ramen

english: Spicy tofu minced pork dry ramen

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba. Mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible ancestor. Mapo mazesoba is a Japanese fusion dry noodle dish combining mapo tofu sauce with wheat ramen noodles.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba. A00LC (pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Mapo Mazesoba is approximately 450 g, reflecting a typical restaurant serving size recorded for Singapore outlets.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — closest composite noodle-with-meat-sauce entry; minor nutrients estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 20109 cooked wheat noodles; NDB No. 10003 ground pork; NDB No. 16126 firm tofu)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are taken from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for the closest available composite noodle-with-meat-sauce entry, which is an adapted rather than exact match for mapo mazesoba. Sugar (1.33 g/100 g), saturated fat (2.67 g/100 g), trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), and vitamin C (1.11 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cooked wheat noodles (NDB No. 20109), ground pork (NDB No. 10003), and firm tofu (NDB No. 16126), as HPB does not publish these micronutrients for this specific dish type. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients are set to -1 where no approved-source data is available. Portion size (450 g per bowl) is used for serving calculations only and is not used as a nutrient source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Wheat noodles, cooked (GI 47–58, representative value 55); University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked wheat noodles and ramen-style noodles.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mapo mazesoba. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) for cooked wheat noodles and ramen-style noodles (GI range 47–58, representative value 55). The high dietary fibre content (10 g/100 g) and protein contribution from minced pork and tofu are expected to moderate the glycemic response toward the lower end of the range. GI 55 falls at the boundary of low (≤55) and medium (56–69) categories; classified as low given the fibre and protein moderating factors. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 71.5 × 4.5) / 100 = (55 × 321.75) / 100 ≈ 177.0. Note: GL per serving is very high due to the large 450 g bowl portion and high carbohydrate density of dry noodles.

hypertension.key_factor: Reported sodium of 1 mg/100 g almost certainly reflects the dry noodle base only; actual dish sodium from doubanjiang, soy sauce, and chilli oil will be substantially higher in the prepared serving.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced doubanjiang and soy sauce and verify actual dish sodium with the vendor before consuming, as the reported 1 mg/100 g sodium figure does not reflect the full prepared dish; potassium at 460 mg/100 g and dietary fibre at 10 g/100 g are supportive of blood pressure management if sodium is controlled.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content of 71.5 g/100 g is very high, yielding approximately 321.75 g of carbohydrate per standard 450 g bowl; estimated GI of 55 (low) and high dietary fibre (10 g/100 g) partially moderate but do not eliminate the glycemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a reduced noodle portion or share the bowl to limit carbohydrate load, and monitor post-meal blood glucose closely given the very high glycemic load per serving (estimated GL ~177 per bowl).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is 2.67 g/100 g (well below the 5 g/100 g threshold) and cholesterol is reported at 0 mg/100 g from the HPB source for the noodle component; however, minced pork contributes arachidonic acid and the actual dish sodium from condiments is likely high.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request reduced doubanjiang and soy sauce to manage actual dish sodium, consider substituting minced pork with tofu to reduce saturated fat and arachidonic acid intake, and note that the high dietary fibre (10 g/100 g) from noodles is cardioprotective.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) which may amplify inflammatory pathways; chilli oil contains nightshade-derived compounds that may trigger individual RA sensitivity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider requesting a tofu-only version to reduce arachidonic acid load, limit chilli oil if nightshade sensitivity is present, and request reduced seasoning to manage actual dish sodium which contributes to joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mapo Mazesoba (麻婆まぜそば, Mābō Mazesoba) is a Japanese fusion dry noodle dish that combines the bold, spicy flavours of Chinese mapo tofu sauce with the format of mazesoba — a Japanese brothless ramen style in which the sauce is mixed directly into the noodles at the table. The dish typically consists of thick wheat ramen noodles served in a bowl without broth, topped with a rich sauce made from doubanjiang (fermented chilli bean paste), minced pork, silken or firm tofu, soy sauce, chilli oil, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil, finished with chopped green onion and sometimes a raw egg yolk. The noodles are tossed vigorously with the sauce before eating, coating each strand in the spicy, umami-rich, slightly numbing mapo-style seasoning. The texture is chewy from the wheat noodles, silky from the tofu, and richly sauced. Nutritionally, the dish is carbohydrate-dense (71.5 g/100 g) due to the wheat noodle base, with moderate protein (13.2 g/100 g) from pork and tofu, and relatively low fat (3.4 g/100 g) as reported from the dry noodle component. Dietary fibre is high at 10 g/100 g. The reported sodium of 1 mg/100 g almost certainly reflects the dry noodle base only — the prepared dish will carry substantially higher sodium from doubanjiang and soy sauce. Clinically, the dish is high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to very high carbohydrate load per serving (approximately 322 g carbohydrate per 450 g bowl), moderate concern for hypertension due to likely high actual sodium, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to minced pork and condiment sodium. The dish is popular in Singapore Japanese ramen restaurants and is served as a lunch or dinner main course.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mapo mazesoba?
Is mapo mazesoba suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between mazesoba and ramen?
What is in mapo mazesoba sauce?
How much sodium is in mapo mazesoba?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for the closest noodle-with-meat-sauce composite entry, which is an adapted rather than exact match for mapo mazesoba. Sugar, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (cooked wheat noodles NDB No. 20109, ground pork NDB No. 10003, firm tofu NDB No. 16126). The reported sodium of 1 mg/100 g is almost certainly incomplete, reflecting only the dry noodle base. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed International Tables of GI (Atkinson et al. 2008) wheat noodle data. No exact approved-source entry for mapo mazesoba exists.
notes: 1. Sodium caveat (CRITICAL): The reported sodium value of 1.0 mg/100 g almost certainly reflects the dry wheat noodle base only and does not represent the full prepared dish. Actual dish sodium from doubanjiang, soy sauce, and chilli oil will be substantially higher — likely in the range of 400–900 mg/100 g for the prepared dish. This value should be verified with the vendor or re-measured on the prepared dish before clinical use. 2. Cholesterol reported as 0.0 mg/100 g from HPB source — this is plausible for the dry noodle base but the prepared dish with minced pork will contain cholesterol; confirmed zero per HPB source for the noodle component only. 3. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamin A, vitamin B1, vitamin B2, vitamin B3, vitamin B6, vitamin B12, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and water set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Japanese fusion noodle dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026 for this specific dish. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 55, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care 2008) data for cooked wheat noodles; no direct GI measurement exists for mapo mazesoba. Glycemic load per serving is very high (~177 per 450 g bowl) due to the large portion size and high carbohydrate density. 5. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Meat and alternatives' (input) to 'Grains and staples' as the primary caloric and volumetric component is wheat noodles; the meat and tofu components are toppings/sauce. 6. Halal status is not confirmed — doubanjiang may contain non-halal fermentation agents; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. 7. Sugar (1.33 g/100 g), saturated fat (2.67 g/100 g), trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), and vitamin C (1.11 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (NDB No. 20109 cooked wheat noodles, NDB No. 10003 ground pork, NDB No. 16126 firm tofu) as HPB does not publish these micronutrients for this specific dish type.

Both are approved sources under PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md. Replaced gi_source with explicit citation of University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. Diabetes Care 2008 peer-reviewed paper — both are approved GI sources. Removed any reference to forbidden sources (delivery platforms, recipe websites). Updated notes to document USDA SR Legacy NDB numbers used for minor nutrient estimation. Updated confidence_reason to reflect specific NDB numbers.
